dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't breathe - this just got really bad today.
How long have you been experiencing this?
For about a week, but it just got so much worse today.
Can you tell me your name?
Alice.
Where are we, Alice?
I think I'm in [CITY].
What is the date today?
Not sure - maybe the 16th?
Can you explain what just happened?
I was feeling more and more out of breath most of the week. I found it hard to walk and feel like I can't catch my breath.
Your eyes appear opened and you are focusing now.
You are responding clearly and answers are reasonable.
Can you squeeze my finger now?
(squeezes)
Good, you follow my instruction well.
Your airway appears clear - are you having any difficulty speaking?
Yes, a little, things feel tight in my throat.
Good, we will monitor it closely.
Your breathing is sounding more rapid than usual, and I see some retractions.
SpO2 is at 85%. Your respiratory rate is 40 per minute.
Partner, what's the blood pressure?
BP is 214 over 113.
Any numbness or tingling in your legs or hands? Is there any leg swelling or discoloration?
I'm fine otherwise. Just out of breath.
Her skin is warm. Pulses are strong and equal bilaterally. No signs of pallor or cyanosis observed.
Great, skin looks good, and circulation is intact so far.
When did your breathing get this bad?
It's been getting worse for about a week now. I feel all week like I can't catch my breath or get enough air.
Have you been doing any new activity or eating something new recently?
No, just feeling sick every day. My regular symptoms have gotten worse.
I see you have a history of asthma, COPD, osteoarthritis, a previous stroke, uterus cancer, kidney disease, and pulmonary hypertension. How are you managing these right now?
I've had them for years. I take my inhalers and also use a portable oxygen too.
How many times have you used the inhaler today?
Three times already, and it's not helping.
Do you have any cough or sputum?
Yes, I cough up pink or red sputum.
How bad is the breathing on a scale from 0 to 10? Any pain at all in your chest or abdomen?
It's a 7 out of 10. I can't really sleep at night due to difficulty breathing, but there's no real chest or abdominal pain that I'm feeling right now.
Can I quickly check your mouth and throat with a light? Just to rule out anything unusual in the airway region.
Go ahead.
You have a hoarse tone of voice, but no visible drainage or obstruction noted right now.
Let's take a quick breath with a stethoscope. If you're okay with it, we'll listen to your lungs.
Sure, anything to help help me figure out why I can't breathe right.
Your breathing is very fast, and we don't have clear or full bilateral breath sounds here. Some signs of labored breathing and wheezing noted.
A 4-lead ECG is being done to check for cardiac involvement, as part of field assessment for a COPD exacerbation.
Heart rate is 115.
Respiratory rate is 40 per minute - that's severely increased for someone this age and clinical picture.
Blood pressure is 214 over 113 sistolic/diastolic. Elevated but at least stable for now.
Pulse ox is 85% - low, but we are monitoring and increasing supplemental oxygen flow as needed.
Blood glucose is not available at the moment, no glucometer results on file yet or tested in the field due to protocol focus on oxygenation and ECG monitoring first.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive bilaterally per documentation and direct observation in the field.
We are managing this for respiratory distress consistent with COPD exacerbation. We are following protocol 3-11 for this case.
Ma'am, I'm going to give you a combination of Albuterol and Ipratropium to help with breathing support.
We are also placing your CPAP now - does that sound okay to you? We will adjust the pressure as per your comfort and breathing effort.
What's in that solution? Is it the inhaler stuff?.
Yes, it's the combination of Albuterol and Ipratropium. We've already given a shot of Dexamethasone earlier to help with inflammation in the airways.
Injection is complete - that should help reduce any airway inflammation observed during physical exam and breath evaluation.
Ma'am, do you feel any better now since we applied the CPAP and administered the medications?.
A bit. I can sort of get air now, and it's easier to sit up in this new position using the mask and CPAP。
Blood pressure is 228 over 118 - that's an increase. I'll note that for reassessment tracking now.
Pulse is 109, andSpO2 is 100% - per documentation a slight improvement in oxygenation here.
Great, your breathing is showing signs of improvement. We'll continue to monitor closely. Keep the CPAP mask on constantly if you can.
Respiratory rate is 29 now - that's a significant drop since the initial evaluation at 40 per minute. A positive sign.
Blood pressure is 217 over 113, heart rate is 108 - show some trend in reduced burden on the cardiovascular system for now..
Oxygen saturation remains at 100% on continuous monitoring. Still good here even if BP is trending slightly higher and heart rate lower.
Let's confirm the CPAP pressure is still set at 5 cm H2O. Any issues with that setting now?.
The latest readings taken show a heart rate of 109, respiratory rate of 29, blood pressure of 228 over 118, and oxygen saturation at 100% - still under field monitoring.
Another check shows a heart rate of 101, respiratory rate of 38, blood pressure of 211 over 119, and oxygen saturation at 89% - some recurrence in deterioration noticed now here
Do you notice any change in your pain or discomfort level since we applied the CPAP and administered the initial interventions?.
It's back to being a struggle - no real pain. I just feel out of breath and the CPAP in combination with meds is not fully working yet.
Great. And your Glasgow Coma Scale is still at 15. You're alert and oriented. No signs of confusion or worsening mental status either documented so far.
We're going to take you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further care, is that okay with you?.
If you think that's the best, yes.
I'll help you transition from the couch to the stretcher now. Are you comfortable with that?.
Okay, do what's best for safety in transport.
We'll secure your harness in a moment. Please stay still for now for comfort and transport safety.
You're fully and safely secured now. We're loading up now. Please stay as still as possible during transport.
We will transfer you to the nurse at [COMPANY_NAME]. They will continue monitoring and treating your COPD exacerbation and breathing issue per trauma bay admission records for Boston Center. .
You'll be in good hands. [COMPANY_NAME] has prior records of your dialysis and chronic pain history to treat appropriately for safety and comfort.
You can use your CPAP while you're there. They can help adjust it if needed upon arrival..
We included details about your kidney disease and current medications, including inhalers and O2, to the receiving nurse at [COMPANY_NAME] for full transfer..
We have everything in motion now. Let's roll. What's the next step?.
She's in a tripoding position, breathing with pursed lips, and requires continuous support with oxygenation and CPAP for effective breathing and stability..
